name: McDonald, Anthony D.
recent_1: Wei, R., McDonald, A. D., Mehta, R. K., & Garcia, A. (2024). Active Inference Models of AV Takeovers: Relating Model Parameters to Trust, Situation Awareness, and Fatigue. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241295932
recent_1_synopsis: Applies active inference (a computational neuroscience framework based on free-energy minimization) to model how drivers take over control from automated vehicles. The study links fitted model parameters to self-reported trust, situation awareness, and cognitive fatigue, showing that interpretable model parameters can stand in for hard-to-measure psychological states. It matters because it offers a principled, mechanistic alternative to black-box prediction of takeover behavior.
recent_2: Parker, J., Lee, J. D., & McDonald, A. D. (2025). Modeling the Impact of Automation on Driver Distraction: A Queuing Theory Approach. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 69(1), 1701-1705. DOI: 10.1177/10711813251383527
recent_2_synopsis: Uses queuing theory to model how partial automation and driver monitoring reshape the allocation of attention between the driving task and non-driving-related tasks. It simulates manual driving, automation-assisted driving, and automation with monitoring prompts to quantify effects on distraction and performance. The work demonstrates an analytic, mechanism-based way to anticipate attentional consequences of automation design choices.
recent_3: Engström, J., Wei, R., McDonald, A. D., Garcia, A., O'Kelly, M., & Johnson, L. (2024). Resolving uncertainty on the fly: modeling adaptive driving behavior as active inference. Frontiers in Neurorobotics, 18, 1341750. DOI: 10.3389/fnbot.2024.1341750
recent_3_synopsis: Develops an active inference account of adaptive human driving behavior, framing drivers as agents that continually resolve perceptual and motor uncertainty as conditions change. The model reproduces how drivers adjust speed and attention under varying uncertainty, providing a generative basis for realistic simulated human drivers. This is significant for building credible human-driver models used to test and validate autonomous vehicles.
seminal_1: McDonald, A. D., Alambeigi, H., Engström, J., Markkula, G., Vogelpohl, T., Dunne, J., & Yuma, N. (2019). Toward Computational Simulations of Behavior During Automated Driving Takeovers: A Review of the Empirical and Modeling Literatures. Human Factors, 61(4), 642-688. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819829572
seminal_1_synopsis: A comprehensive review synthesizing the empirical and modeling literatures on driver behavior during automated-vehicle takeovers, identifying which factors (time budget, repeated exposure, secondary-task type, takeover-request modality, trust, fatigue) drive takeover timing and quality. It lays out a research agenda for computational simulation of takeover behavior. With roughly 262 citations (Semantic Scholar, June 2026), it is foundational because it consolidated a fragmented field and set the modeling agenda for AV takeover research.
seminal_2: McDonald, A. D., Lee, J. D., Schwarz, C., & Brown, T. L. (2014). Steering in a Random Forest: Ensemble Learning for Detecting Drowsiness-Related Lane Departures. Human Factors, 56(5), 986-998. DOI: 10.1177/0018720813515272
seminal_2_synopsis: Introduces a random-forest ensemble-learning algorithm that detects drowsiness-related lane departures from steering-wheel-angle data, outperforming the established PERCLOS eye-based measure in classification accuracy and AUC. It was an early, influential demonstration that machine learning on vehicle-based signals can detect impaired-driver states. With approximately 72 citations (Semantic Scholar, June 2026), it helped establish McDonald's machine-learning-for-driver-state line of work.
expertise_overview: Anthony D. McDonald's research centers on driving safety, human-automation interaction, and the modeling of human driver behavior. A core thread is detecting and predicting driver state, such as drowsiness, distraction, and fatigue, from vehicle-based and physiological signals using machine learning methods including random forests and other classifiers. A second thread is computational and cognitive modeling of behavior during automated-vehicle takeovers, increasingly using active inference and other generative frameworks to produce interpretable, mechanistic models of attention, trust, and situation awareness. His methods span machine learning, statistical and Bayesian analysis, queuing theory, and driving-simulator and naturalistic-driving experiments. Application domains include automated and conditionally automated vehicles, driver monitoring systems, and the development of realistic simulated human drivers for AV testing and validation. He also applies these behavioral-modeling and physiological-signal methods to adjacent human-factors problems such as PTSD and operator performance.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Anthony D. McDonald is an industrial/systems engineering researcher in driving safety, machine learning, and automated vehicles, currently at the University of Wisconsin-Madison (Industrial and Systems Engineering) and formerly at Texas A&M University; frequent co-author with John D. Lee (UW-Madison). Identity corroborated by Frontiers in Neurorobotics author list, Sage/Human Factors author pages, Texas A&M Scholars (vivo.library.tamu.edu) profile, and Semantic Scholar. Google Scholar profile could not be fetched directly (login redirect), so most-cited papers were identified via Semantic Scholar and web search rather than Scholar's ranking. All DOIs verified via Crossref on 2026-06-17: 10.1177/00187208241295932 (Active Inference Models of AV Takeovers, Human Factors), 10.1177/10711813251383527 (Queuing Theory, HFES Proceedings 69(1):1701-1705, 2025), 10.3389/fnbot.2024.1341750 (Resolving uncertainty on the fly, Frontiers in Neurorobotics 18, 2024), 10.1177/0018720819829572 (Toward Computational Simulations, pre-verified), 10.1177/0018720813515272 (Steering in a Random Forest, Human Factors 56(5):986-998). Citation counts for the two seminal papers are from the Semantic Scholar API on 2026-06-17: 262 (Toward Computational Simulations) and 72 (Steering in a Random Forest). No citations are unverified; every DOI resolved with matching title and metadata.
